metadata.archetype_justification: Graylog owns persistent state in MongoDB (users, streams, dashboards, event definitions, content packs) and exposes full CRUD operations via 187+ JAX-RS REST endpoints across 14 resource groups.

findings.title: No Audit Logging in Open-Source Edition
findings.description: The open-source edition ships with NullAuditEventSender — a no-op implementation that silently discards all audit events. While the audit framework defines 100+ event types and integrates with REST endpoints via @AuditEvent annotations, no audit events are actually persisted.
findings.gap: No audit trail whatsoever for any operation — read or write — in the open-source deployment. Agent-initiated requests cannot be attributed or forensically traced.
findings.recommendation: Implement a MongoAuditEventSender that persists audit events to a dedicated MongoDB collection. The framework already defines all event types and integrations — only the sender implementation needs replacing.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to BLOCKER: agent_scope=read-only but the system has NO audit logging at all (NullAuditEventSender), which is a BLOCKER regardless of scope.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: AUTH-Q6 base severity for read-only is RISK-SAFETY, but the complete absence of any audit logging (not even mutable logging exists) escalates to BLOCKER. You cannot attribute any operation to any principal.

blocker_remediation.immediate: Implement a MongoAuditEventSender that persists audit events to a dedicated MongoDB collection.
blocker_remediation.target_state: All API operations log the authenticated principal, action, resource, and timestamp to an append-only audit collection.

findings.title: No Pre-Built Agent Role Template for Least Privilege
findings.description: Graylog implements a fine-grained permission model with 100+ permissions in domain:action format and per-instance scoping. However, there is no dedicated agent role template with minimal permissions pre-configured.
findings.gap: No documented guidance or pre-built role for agent identities with least-privilege defaults. An admin creating an agent token must manually determine the minimal permission set.
findings.recommendation: Define and document a least-privilege agent role template that restricts agent tokens to only the API operations required for the intended use case.

findings.title: Admin Bypass Circumvents Action-Level Authorization
findings.description: The permission model supports action-level granularity (streams:read vs streams:edit vs streams:delete). However, the coarse admin role bypasses all action-level checks — admin tokens have unrestricted access.
findings.gap: No mechanism to restrict an admin-created agent token to read-only operations if the creating user is an admin. The admin bypass is a blast-radius risk.
findings.recommendation: Enforce that agent tokens cannot inherit admin privileges regardless of the creating user's role. Add token-level permission restriction.

findings.title: No Single-Operation Agent Identity Suspension
findings.description: UserSessionTerminationService supports global and per-user session termination. Access tokens can be individually deleted. However, there is no API endpoint to immediately revoke a specific access token by token value without knowing which user owns it.
findings.gap: No single kill switch API to suspend a specific agent identity by token identifier. Suspension requires multi-step process with potential delay.
findings.recommendation: Implement a token revocation endpoint that accepts a token identifier and immediately invalidates it across all cluster nodes.

findings.title: No API-Layer Rate Limiting
findings.description: No API-layer rate limiting exists in the application. No rate limiting middleware, throttling configuration, 429 response handling, or rate limit headers were found.
findings.gap: A runaway agent loop could make thousands of API calls per second with no throttling. No defense against agent-induced traffic storms.
findings.recommendation: Implement per-token rate limiting at the Jersey filter level with configurable limits per permission role. Return 429 with Retry-After header.

findings.title: No PII Redaction in Application Logs
findings.description: Logging uses Log4j2 with plain-text PatternLayout. No PII scrubbing, masking, or redaction filters exist in the logging pipeline.
findings.gap: No mechanism to prevent PII from appearing in application logs. Agent-initiated requests that trigger error paths may log request details including user-related fields.
findings.recommendation: Add a Log4j2 RewritePolicy or custom LogEvent filter that redacts known PII patterns. Consider switching to JsonTemplateLayout for structured logging.

findings.title: No Complete Version-Controlled API Specification
findings.description: One hand-authored OpenAPI 3.1.0 spec exists covering only stream destination filter endpoints. Build-time generation exists but output is not committed.
findings.gap: No complete, version-controlled machine-readable API specification for the 187+ REST endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI step that generates and commits the OpenAPI spec on each release.

findings.title: No Machine-Readable Error Classification in Responses
findings.description: Well-structured error hierarchy with GenericError interface and 14 ExceptionMapper classes. Missing retryable flag and error code enumeration.
findings.gap: Error responses lack a retryable field or error code that agents can use to distinguish transient from permanent errors.
findings.recommendation: Add a code field (string enum) and retryable boolean to the GenericError interface.

findings.title: Optional Tracing and Unstructured Logging
findings.description: OpenTelemetry integration exists via external Java agent (no-op fallback). Logging is plain-text with no correlation IDs or trace context injection.
findings.gap: Traces are optional; logs lack correlation IDs. Agent-initiated requests cannot be easily traced end-to-end.
findings.recommendation: Enable OTel auto-instrumentation for JAX-RS endpoints and switch Log4j2 to JsonTemplateLayout with trace context fields.

findings.title: No Alerting Configuration for API Metrics
findings.description: 100+ Prometheus metrics configured covering streams, inputs, buffers, journal, search/indexing. No alerting rules or thresholds defined.
findings.gap: Metrics are collected but no alerting thresholds configured within the application.
findings.recommendation: Add reference Prometheus alerting rule configuration for API latency, error rates, and throughput anomalies.

findings.title: No API Versioning or Contract Testing
findings.description: No URL-based API versioning, Accept-Version headers, schema registry, or breaking change detection in CI.
findings.gap: Agent tool bindings could break silently when the API changes between versions.
findings.recommendation: Add OpenAPI spec comparison (oasdiff) to CI pipeline to detect and flag breaking changes.

findings.title: No Infrastructure as Code in Repository
findings.description: No Terraform, CloudFormation, CDK, Helm charts, or Kubernetes manifests present. Deployment infrastructure managed externally.
findings.gap: Agent-facing infrastructure not defined or governed in this repository.
findings.recommendation: Add reference deployment configuration documenting expected production topology.

findings.title: No API Contract Testing in CI Pipeline
findings.description: GitHub Actions CI runs Maven verify with unit and integration tests. No API contract testing, OpenAPI diff, or schema validation in pipeline.
findings.gap: CI pipeline does not detect API-breaking changes.
findings.recommendation: Add CI step that generates and diffs OpenAPI spec against committed baseline.

findings.title: No Deployment Rollback Mechanism in Repository
findings.description: Database migrations are forward-only (75 migrations, no downgrade). No deployment rollback mechanisms defined in repository.
findings.gap: A broken release cannot be quickly rolled back from within this repository.
findings.recommendation: Document rollback procedure and ensure external deployment supports one-click rollback.

findings.title: No Production-Equivalent Staging Environment Configuration
findings.description: Docker Compose files exist for migration testing. Testcontainers-based integration tests provide development environments. No production-equivalent staging with seed data.
findings.gap: No documented staging environment with production-equivalent data shape for agent testing.
findings.recommendation: Provide Docker Compose configuration with seed data for agent integration testing.

findings.title: No Idempotency Key Support in Write Endpoints
findings.description: No idempotency key support exists in write endpoints. POST operations (creating streams, users, dashboards) do not support idempotency keys or duplicate request detection.
findings.gap: Non-idempotent write operations. Becomes BLOCKER under write-enabled scope.
findings.recommendation: Implement idempotency key support (via Idempotency-Key header) on critical write endpoints before expanding agent scope to write-enabled.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: agent_scope=read-only — read-only agents do not execute write operations.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: API-Q4 conditional BLOCKER resolves to INFO for read-only scope because agents will not invoke write endpoints.

findings.title: Structured JSON Response Format
findings.description: All API responses use structured JSON (Jackson serialization). Content-Type is application/json. DTOs are well-defined with AutoValue/Jackson annotations. Pagination responses wrap entities in a standard structure with metadata.
findings.gap: None — JSON is the optimal format for agent consumption.
findings.recommendation: No action needed.

findings.title: Robust Event Emission for State Changes
findings.description: Graylog has robust event emission capabilities: internal EventBus for state changes, outbound HTTP notifications (HTTPEventNotificationV2), Slack/Teams webhooks, and configurable event definitions that trigger notifications on log-based conditions.
findings.gap: None — event emission capability is mature.
findings.recommendation: Document webhook payload schemas for agent consumption.

findings.title: No Rate Limit Documentation or Headers
findings.description: No rate limit documentation exists and no rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After) are returned in API responses. The application does not enforce or communicate rate limits.
findings.gap: Agents cannot self-throttle based on rate limit signals.
findings.recommendation: When rate limiting is implemented (see STATE-Q5), include standard rate limit headers in all API responses.

findings.title: No Identity Propagation or On-Behalf-Of Flow
findings.description: Graylog's access token system authenticates the token bearer as the owning user. There is no OAuth2 on-behalf-of flow or token exchange pattern. The system cannot distinguish agent acting as itself vs agent acting on behalf of user X at the protocol level.
findings.gap: Cannot distinguish agent-as-self vs agent-on-behalf-of-user.
findings.recommendation: For read-only agent use cases, a dedicated agent user account with a scoped token is sufficient.

findings.title: Credential Management Without External Key Store
findings.description: Access tokens are encrypted at rest using AES-SIV (RFC 5297) in MongoDB. General secrets use AES-CBC encryption via EncryptedValueService. The master encryption key (password_secret) is stored in the graylog.conf configuration file. No HSM or external key management system integration exists.
findings.gap: No external key management integration (Vault, AWS KMS).
findings.recommendation: Consider Vault or AWS Secrets Manager integration for the password_secret and other sensitive configuration values in production deployments.

findings.title: Limited Compensation and Rollback Capability
findings.description: Content pack operations support rollback on failure. Individual entity handlers implement compensation logic. However, no general-purpose saga pattern or multi-step transaction compensation framework exists for arbitrary API operations.
findings.gap: No general multi-step compensation framework. Becomes BLOCKER under write-enabled scope.
findings.recommendation: Before expanding to write-enabled scope, implement compensation endpoints or saga coordination for critical multi-step workflows.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: agent_scope=read-only — read-only agents do not execute multi-step write workflows.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: STATE-Q1 conditional BLOCKER resolves to INFO for read-only scope because read-only agents do not perform state-mutating multi-step operations.

findings.title: No Application-Level Optimistic Locking on REST Endpoints
findings.description: MongoDB atomic operations (findAndModify, conditional updates) are used in some areas. No application-level optimistic locking (ETags, version fields) is implemented on REST endpoints.
findings.gap: No ETag/version field support on write endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Add version fields and If-Match ETag support on write endpoints before enabling write scope for agents.

findings.title: No Configurable Transaction Limits Per Agent Identity
findings.description: No configurable transaction limits exist per agent identity. No maximum records per operation, maximum spend per hour, or similar blast-radius controls are implemented.
findings.gap: No blast-radius controls for agent operations.
findings.recommendation: Implement per-token operation limits before expanding to write-enabled scope.

findings.title: No Draft/Pending State for Entities
findings.description: No draft/pending state exists for most entity types. Content packs have a versioning/installation model that provides review before activation, but standard entities (streams, dashboards, pipelines) are immediately active on creation.
findings.gap: No draft/pending state for standard entities.
findings.recommendation: Consider a draft state for agent-created entities before expanding to write-enabled scope.

findings.title: No Configurable Approval Gates
findings.description: No configurable approval gates exist for high-risk operations. All authorized API operations execute immediately.
findings.gap: No human-in-the-loop mechanism at the application layer.
findings.recommendation: Add operation-level approval requirements (configurable per operation type) before enabling write scope.

findings.title: Sensitive Data Classification — Practical Controls Present
findings.description: Stage A = Yes (system stores user-specific fields). Stage B: B1 (API response scoping) — CLEAR: Password hashes excluded from API responses via separate DTO layer. B2 (Access control differentiation) — CLEAR: Permission model distinguishes users:read from users:edit with per-instance scoping. B3 (Formal classification metadata) — INFO: No field-level sensitivity annotations exist.
findings.gap: No formal data classification metadata (B3 = INFO only). Practical controls (B1, B2) are in place.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding field-level sensitivity annotations for documentation purposes.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: B1 CLEAR, B2 CLEAR, only B3 (formal classification metadata) absent — overall DATA-Q1 = INFO.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: DATA-Q1 resolves to INFO because the practical data protection controls (B1 response scoping and B2 access differentiation) are present. Only the aspirational B3 (formal classification metadata) is absent.

b1.reasoning: Password hashes excluded from API responses via separate DTO layer (UserSummary). Access tokens not returned in list responses.

b2.reasoning: Permission model distinguishes users:read from users:edit with per-instance scoping.

b3.reasoning: No field-level sensitivity annotations or data classification tags exist in the codebase.

findings.title: Data Residency — Self-Hosted Operator Controlled
findings.description: No GDPR, data residency, or data sovereignty configuration exists in the codebase. No region-specific data routing. The system is self-hosted, meaning data residency is determined by the operator's deployment location.
findings.gap: No application-level residency controls (operator responsibility).
findings.recommendation: Document data residency requirements in the deployment guide for organizations using Graylog with AI agents.

ara_metadata.conditional_resolution: Resolved to INFO: agent_scope=read-only and system is self-hosted. Data residency is operator-controlled at deployment time.
ara_metadata.resolution_reasoning: DATA-Q2 conditional BLOCKER base severity for read-only is RISK-SAFETY, further calibrated to INFO because the self-hosted deployment model means data residency is entirely operator-controlled — no application-level gap exists.

findings.title: No Data Quality Scoring or Completeness Metrics
findings.description: No formal data quality scoring, completeness metrics, or data profiling exists. The system processes incoming log data without quality analysis. Indexer failure handling exists (failure indexer, dead letter patterns) for processing failures.
findings.gap: No data quality metadata.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding data freshness indicators to search responses (latest indexed timestamp, index lag metrics).

findings.title: Semantically Meaningful Field Names
findings.description: Field names are human-readable and semantically meaningful across the codebase. API responses use descriptive names (created_at, stream_rules, message_count, event_definitions). No legacy abbreviation patterns detected.
findings.gap: None — field naming is already agent-friendly.
findings.recommendation: No action needed.

findings.title: MCP Server as Lightweight Service Catalog
findings.description: No formal data catalog exists. The MCP server's list_resources and read_resource tools provide a programmatic discovery mechanism for streams, dashboards, and event definitions. The OpenAPI generation capability provides schema documentation at build time.
findings.gap: No external metadata layer (Glue, DataHub) integration.
findings.recommendation: Leverage the MCP server's resource listing as the agent-facing catalog. Consider publishing entity schemas to a service catalog.

findings.title: No Business Outcome Metrics
findings.description: Prometheus metrics cover infrastructure and processing metrics (message throughput, buffer utilization, indexing rates, search latency). Custom business-outcome metrics are not emitted by the application itself.
findings.gap: No agent-specific success metrics.
findings.recommendation: Define agent-specific success metrics externally (e.g., percentage of agent queries returning non-empty results).
